speaker: interviewer
text: [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, the camera's running.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's October the 25th, 1996. The survivor is Vera Gissing, maiden name Diamant. The interviewer is Bernice Krantz. We are in Marlowe, England, and the language is English.

speaker: interviewer
text: Camera's running.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's October the 25th, 1996. I'm conducting an interview with Vera Gissing. My name is Bernice Krantz, and we're in Wargrave, Berkshire.

speaker: interviewer
text: Camera's running.

speaker: interviewer
text: Please can you give me your name and spell it?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Vera Gissing. Spelled G-I-S-S-I-N-G.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you give me your maiden name, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was Diamant. And that is spelled D-I-A-M-A-N-T.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever have any nicknames?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really, though some children when I was little and I cried, they used to call me Pearl, because my tears were supposed to be pearls, as I was a diamond.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. And can you please give me your date of birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: 4Th of July, 1928.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Prague, in Czechoslovakia.

speaker: interviewer
text: And right. If we could just turn off the tape for a minute.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's useless. I think we've done it before.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you like to introduce your family for us, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: This young lady on my right side is called Rebecca, and this granddaughter of mine-- they're both my granddaughters-- is called Lauren.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: And behind me is my eldest daughter, Nicola, their mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Nicola, is there anything you'd like to say about your mother and how you feel your life may have been different if she hadn't gone through what she went through as a child?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I was a child, I was always aware that there's something very different about our family compared my friends' families, although I couldn't really understand what it was. We had an aunt that used to come stay with us every year from Czechoslovakia, our Auntie Berta, who survived the camps. And although she didn't talk about it, again I was aware that something terrible had happened to her. And she used to show us her-- her numbers that were printed on her arm. And I was always very frightened, very frightened about the war. But didn't really understand the sort of implications.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was it that you began to realize what it was all about?

speaker: interviewee
text: Probably as we got older. I think we, as children, we understood. We understood Mum had had a-- you know, terrible experience as a child, and that it had affected-- affected her very much. But as a child yourself, you just sort of-- you know, you don't understand it, and you're scared about it. Scared about the war and what might happen to you, because it happened to her.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know, when you were a child, you mother's story?

speaker: interviewee
text: I did. And I don't think Mum realized, actually, how scared I was. I had a supply of things I kept in my room, in case it happened to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Such as?

speaker: interviewee
text: A sort of hidey hole at the back of the airing cupboard. My escape route if there was ever another war.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what had you put in there as--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, just little sort of belongings. And I had this plan to go to Switzerland, because it was a neutral country. Because they used to talk about the war, and the camps, and not in a way to frighten me, but just to inform us. I was always scared that it might happen again. We might get separated and our parents taken away.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you did know at a young age that your mother had to come here on her own?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. And they didn't tell us about the atrocities, not in any such graphic detail. But I think having our-- our auntie live with us, you could see how much she had suffered and how, you know, marked she was by it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you feel that you protected or tried to protect the children against what you'd been through, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't try and protect them. That, I think, isn't the appropriate word in my case. I perhaps over informed them, when I hear now how scared Nikki was, for instance. But I didn't realize it at the time. But what I tried to do is to let them grow up as independently as possible. Because, again, I was afraid, not in case there was another Holocaust, but that in case perhaps I was killed with my husband in a plane crash or something, that they wouldn't be tied too much to my apron strings. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I think that's about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything you two young ladies would like to say?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, go on.

speaker: interviewee
text: About your famous granny?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, tell them I come to your school. Go on. You tell them. Sometimes I go to their school to talk about, you know, the past. And I talk to children as young as Rebecca, nine years old. But of course, again, I minimize the horror and make it acceptable to that-- to that age group. They're normally very talkative young ladies, but I think they lost their tongue today. [CHUCKLES]

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything else that you'd like to add?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really. I remember, as a child, being confused about what it meant to be Jewish, because Mum used to sometimes say, you realize you're Jewish? And then everything became much more difficult when I was about 15 and had my first boyfriend, who was German. And you know, Mum couldn't cope with it. It was very difficult. And she would remind me occasionally I was Jewish, but I didn't know what it meant. And yet I knew that it meant an awful lot to her, and she lost her parents because of it. And again, I didn't really-- you know, understand. And at that point, I would say, well, this is my life, and you can't sort of live your life through me. But it was a difficult situation for her. And I probably didn't fully realize till I was older-- I went to Israel-- just what people had-- had lost and suffered.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Thanks very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Camera's running.

speaker: interviewer
text: If we can start by talking about your family and your early childhood.

speaker: interviewee
text: By all means. My father was called Karel, which is Charles in English. He was a very handsome, tall man and a factory owner. We had wine and spirit manufacture. Manufacture and import and export. The office was part of our household. We had a house in which we lived, and we had several houses built around the courtyard where there was the office and-- and all the cellars and everything that went with the factory. My mother ran the office. And she was a beautiful woman, very soft and very warm. And though I loved them both, I loved her desperately. I mean, there was a very special closeness between her and I. Her name was Irma. She had beautiful, golden hazel-colored hair. And again, she was tall and a very dignified lady. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My sister was close to my father. She was 4 and 1/2 years my senior, and her name was Eva. I used to envy her because she was very elegant and very grown up, whereas I was the little tomboy who always got into scrapes, and always got dirty, and always got told off, and moreover, always had to make do with her castoffs, where she got all the new clothes and all the new bicycles. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I had no more brothers and sisters, but we were a very close, very happy family, living in a little town on the outskirts of Prague, where there were beautiful woods to romp around in and rivers to bathe in. And-- and apart from school, there was a lovely gymnasium which I attended every-- every week. And I had loads of friends. But my bosom friend was Marta. She was a daughter of a village cobbler and came from a completely different background and was Catholic, whereas I was Jewish of course. But there were only a handful of families of Jewish faith in our little town. And in Czechoslovakia, which was such a new country-- I mean, after all, it was only founded in 1918, after the end of the First World War-- and I was really a child of the first generation. And I was fiercely proud of being a Czech, and fiercely patriotic. And it mattered much more than religion. Religion didn't seem to be of any consequence. I always felt what was important is how you lived, not how and where you worshipped. My mother, every Christmas time, for instance, used to have a grand bake. And she was a fantastic cook. And she made little parcels of the goodies which she baked and then gave them to Eva and me to distribute to the poor and the lonely throughout our town. And she did this religiously every-- every year. And as to this day is-- remem-- is remembered for this with great fondness. And my father, too, was looked upon as a wise man. And he used to love playing cards in the local pub. And every Saturday-- no, not Saturday. Every Sunday morning, people used to flock to the pub who needed advice, because he always had a session. He was always ready to help anyone who needed help. So I grew up in this atmosphere, which I think sort of laid very strong foundations and have remained not just as a memory, but as a part of me all my life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me the name of this little town that you lived in?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was called, or it is called still today, Celakovice.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell that, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: If you want it spelled, C-E-L-A-K-O-V-I-C-E.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have grandparents?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had maternal grandparents. My father's parents had died, before I was born, of natural causes. My grandmother was blind. She had been blinded by accident during an operation [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] when she was very young. Blind still, but already had four children. And as my mother was the eldest, she took over, really, the responsibility of-- of running the family. And my grandmother, though she could not see, saw with her-- with her hands, and I will never forget her. The gentleness of her hands when she stroked my face and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] remarked, oh, you've grown. Or you know, you've-- your hair is longer. And things like that. She never let us sort of feel that she was in any way handicapped.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name was Matilda. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The first memory, funnily enough, of myself as a little girl, is of me leading her by the hand across the courtyard to my mother's office. It was the very first time I was entrusted with such responsibility. And I was maybe five years old and felt very important to do this. And unfortunately, there was a-- sort of a stone jutting out in the yard, which I didn't see. And she tripped over it and fell. And her face bled. And I ran off, absolutely hysterically terrified. I didn't even dare go immediately to my mother's office, because I was so afraid of what she would tell me. But anyway, it all ended, obviously, happily, without me being attributed any blame. But to this day, I can still feel the fear and the guilt of that little child who-- who I was then. My grandfather, he was a businessman, but he was also a concert pianist and one of the founding members of the Social Democratic Party. So quite a mixture. What I remember mostly about him is his beard, because he had such a thick, lovely, reddish beard, which used to tickle abominably every time he asked for a kiss. He didn't like coming to stay with us very much, because his name was Julius, or Julius. I, in his honor, named our cat Julius. I thought he'd be very proud, because I loved the cat. But he took it as a bit of an insult. [CHUCKLES] But he was a-- he was a very, very dignified, wonderful man. And we used to go to-- my mother and father-- every Sunday for lunch to Prague with-- with-- so with my grandmother and grandfather every Sunday.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a long way?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not too long. It was about an hour from door to door. We always went by the local train, which still runs today. The same old carriages and the same old hard wooden seats. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] During our lunch, we were always joined by my mother's brother, Gustav, and his wife Marta. Gustav was a lawyer, and they lived in Prague. And they had two sons, similar ages to my sister and I. The oldest was called Tomas, and the youngest was-- sorry, the oldest was called Hanosh, Hans. And the youngest was Tommy, Tomas. And I always envied them because they looked immaculate. They were so well-mannered. Real town boys, whereas as I was a real country tomboy. We were such a contrast. But [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] those family Sundays, I didn't particularly like them, because I had-- I had to keep still for so long. And I always had a problem in keeping the food on my plate instead of on my lap. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But I came to miss them very much when they stopped.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about schooling?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to the ordinary school in Celakovice. All the schools were state schools except in Prague, there was a German school, and I think there was an English college, but for the older ones. There were no Jewish schools. There wasn't even synagogue in-- in our town. We had a visiting rabbi who came, I think, once a fortnight to give us a bit of a lesson, you know, all the few Jewish children who were there. But quite frankly, when it came to religion being taught, I-- I just stayed. I didn't even have the option of not sitting there. And as I said earlier, it didn't matter to me. It was all a part of learning. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: So when you say you stayed, where did you stay?

speaker: interviewee
text: At the school, when they had a religious lesson.

speaker: interviewer
text: The rabbi came to your school to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, no. The rabbi did not come to the school. Because when I say there were about six Jewish children in the town, they were from different schools. In my school, I think there were only two of us. The others were in the gymnasium, which of course was for older children from-- from 11 onwards.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you meet with the rabbi?

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- in-- a-- a house of one of the families. We took it in turns. So this happened, as I said, about once a fortnight.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your sister attend with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, my sister always attended.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was a mixture of ages.

speaker: interviewee
text: Very much so, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your mother light candles on a Friday night?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We did do that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was an awareness.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was an awareness. And-- but there wasn't sort of a religious awareness. You know, we were not observant Jewish families. But you will find that very few Jews who lived in the-- in Bohemia were observant. Those who lived in Slovakia and in the further regions bordering on Russia and Poland, that they were-- quite a lot of them were Orthodox. And in Moravia, some of them were very observant. But in Bohemia, there were very few. I think a lot-- it had a lot to do with Czechoslovakia, as I said, being a new state. We also had a wonderful president, Tomas Garrigue Masaryk, who was a friend of the Jews and made no discriminations. And it was wonderful to work and to study and to live in such an environment, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you aware of Hitler's rise to power?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not until 1938. In the-- I mean, even then, I was nine years old. You know, it's-- I mean, when you're eight or seven, and there's no television or anything like that, names like Hitler and that-- it doesn't mean much to you. But in '38, when first Austria was taken over, and then of course, when Sudetenland [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] was taken by-- by the Germans, and in fact, Czechoslovakia was forced to cede it to Germany because both France and England, who were our allies, forbade us to go to war. They said this would be our price for conserving world peace, by giving this region to Germany, because they felt Hitler's appetite would be appeased by gaining it. And of course that didn't happen. But after that, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] many thousands and thousands of refugees fled to Prague and the regions around Prague. And they were not just refugees from Sudetenland, where there were many people who were political enemies of Hitler, as well as Jews. And there were also many people who'd fled there from Germany and Austria. So there was a real mixture. And it was a terrible, terrible difficult thing to deal with them, to house them, even in makeshift camps, and to finance their keep. But I always remember that just after-- it was in the autumn when Sudetenland [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] came into German hands-- and the next day, when I came to school, there was a girl in our class, a girl I had never seen. And she was without any shoes and without a coat. And it was a chilly autumn day. And I asked her how come that she's not dressed properly. And she said to me that they-- the family had to flee in the middle of the night, and that her father just gathered her in his arms and rushed her to the car, and then they drove. And it wasn't till they got to their friends in Celakovice that they realized, you know, that-- that-- that she had just no clothes at all. And impulsively, I took my shoes off. I realized her feet were about the same size as mine, and said, take them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I lived just across the road from the school. And I ran in break time home and got myself another pair. And my mother came into the bedroom as I was doing so, and I felt very guilty. She said, what on earth are you doing? You know. Where are your shoes? And I felt really bad about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: But when I explained, she just hugged me tight and said, bring-- I can't remember the girl's name now-- but bring your new friend back here after school, and I'll see what we can find. And she ended up with a coat and with lots of clothes. And that Christmas, she and her parents ended up at our house to celebrate. Well, we didn't celebrate in the Christian way, but we had presents, and they got presents with us. And this is the sort of a person, you know, my parents were, anyway. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The next thing I remember [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that my parents' faces got more and more anxious as time progressed. And our cellar and our pantry got fuller and fuller with all sort of food which could be stored.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ask questions?

speaker: interviewee
text: I asked questions, and my mother always sort of just smiled and said, well, you never know when they will come in handy. They did not wish to frighten us, or frighten me, because I am quite convinced my sister, who was nearly five years older than I, obviously was more aware of what might happen. And then one day-- it was the 15th of March, 1939-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I woke up to the sound, strange sound, coming from the street. And as I opened my eyes, I saw my mother and father and my sister standing by the open window, looking down into the street. And there were tears running down their faces. And I crept up to their side, and there were German soldiers goose-marching into the square in front of our house.

speaker: interviewer
text: I remember it was snowing, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and the streets were lined with people. And there was an utter silence. And then one person started singing the Czech national anthem, which starts with the worlds-- words, where is my home?

speaker: interviewee
text: And everyone joined in. Our little family joined in. And as we sang where is our home, I wondered what was happening to our dear home. And that really shook me. But you know, at 10 years old, one is quite resilient. And when, a few hours later, our maid-- whom we sent out to have a look because there was a notice pinned to the door of our school-- the maid came back to say that the school is closed till further notice. I couldn't help but feeling quite pleased that we were in for an unexpected holiday. I mean, after all, I was only 10 years old, barely that. But soon after that, I think it was the very next day, a German soldier came and knocked on our door. And he said, I hear that you've got a separate room with a separate entrance in your flat. And my father said, yes, that is correct. And the soldier said, our commandant wants it for his office. Let me see it. And so my parents took him to see the room, which was used only for special occasions and business entertaining. It housed wonderful furniture, which I was told that a count bought for his bride to be. It was all hand carved. And then the bride to be suddenly died, and so it went for sale, and my father bought it for mother. And it also housed my mother's prec-- my mother's precious china collection, and a grand piano which my grandfather had made for us. And various things like that, and rugs my mother had made. And anyway, this room we had to give to the commandant.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm sorry to interrupt you there, but the tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: I know. Yes, I realize. Yes.
